skill.name: ml-expert
skill.description: Implement machine learning models, training pipelines, optimization changes, and validation workflows with a specialist PyTorch-focused agent.
skill.summary: Build and improve PyTorch machine learning systems with implementation plans, tests, and performance checks.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but the weak-crypto and network-reconnaissance hits are false positives from ML terminology and fenced examples. The confirmed risk is operational: the documented SDK configuration grants a spawned specialist agent Write, Edit, and Bash tools, so publication should include a warning about file modification and command execution.

medium_findings.title: Spawned Agent Has File Editing and Bash Tool Access
medium_findings.description: The skill documentation shows a ClaudeAgentOptions configuration that enables Write, Edit, and Bash for the specialist agent. This is appropriate for an implementation skill, but it can modify project files and run commands if a user invokes it on untrusted repositories or prompts.
medium_findings.recommendation: Publish with a marketplace warning and require users to review plans before allowing edits or command execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The tool list explicitly includes Bash, Write, and Edit, which confirms an elevated operational capability. The documented permission mode is plan, so there is no evidence of malicious automation or silent execution.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Network Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The high weak-cryptography hits align with ML-related text and example snippets, not cryptographic APIs or hashing logic. The low network-reconnaissance hits also appear in ordinary ML implementation context rather than scanning or discovery behavior.
low_findings.recommendation: No security block is needed for these static hits; keep the command-capability warning separate.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found ML descriptions, JSON examples, and Python examples at the cited areas, with no cryptographic algorithm use or reconnaissance intent. Confidence is high because the reviewed context explains the static matches without indicating malware.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Enabled for Implementation Agent
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash to a spawned agent so it can run tests, profiling, and validation commands. This is useful but should be treated as command-execution capability in marketplace risk labeling.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Keep plan-mode review enabled and ask users to inspect proposed commands before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Bash is explicitly listed as an allowed tool, and the agent prompt discusses running validation. No evidence found that commands are hardcoded for exfiltration or persistence.

content.user_title: Build PyTorch Models With an ML Specialist
content.value_statement: Machine learning implementation often needs architecture planning, careful training code, and reliable validation. This skill guides a specialist agent to build PyTorch models, tests, documentation, and performance checks.
actual_capabilities: Plans model architecture components before implementation.
Implements PyTorch modules, training loops, and configuration classes.
Adds unit, integration, shape, gradient, and parameter-count tests.
Optimizes inference speed, memory use, and numerical stability.
Documents architecture decisions, usage examples, and performance metrics.
limitations: Requires clear requirements, constraints, and reference material for complex papers.
May need user approval before editing files or running commands.
Focuses on PyTorch and may not fit non-Python ML stacks.
Cannot guarantee production accuracy without real data and domain validation.

use_cases.title: Implement a New Research Model
use_cases.description: Turn architecture requirements and papers into PyTorch modules, configs, tests, and usage examples.

use_cases.title: Repair Training Instability
use_cases.description: Apply fixes for gradient flow, loss design, checkpointing, and validation problems in existing training code.

use_cases.title: Optimize Model Runtime
use_cases.description: Improve inference speed and memory behavior while preserving tests and documented performance metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Model
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small PyTorch module.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ml-expert to implement a PyTorch classifier for my dataset. Include a config class, forward pass, and basic shape tests.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Training Behavior
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate debugging of an existing training issue.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ml-expert to review this training code and fix unstable loss behavior. Add tests that prove gradients flow correctly.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Training Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ml-expert to build a training pipeline with checkpointing, validation metrics, mixed precision, and resume support.

prompt_templates.title: Implement a Paper Under Constraints
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced architecture implementation with resource limits.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ml-expert to implement this architecture from the attached paper with a 25M parameter target and a 6GB VRAM limit.

output_examples.input: Implement a compact transformer model with a 25M parameter budget.
output: A component plan with parameter estimates for embeddings, transformer layers, and output heads.
PyTorch model files with configuration classes and documented forward passes.
Tests for shapes, gradients, parameter count, and a small end-to-end training check.

output_examples.input: Optimize my model because inference is too slow for real-time use.
output: A profiling-focused implementation plan that identifies likely bottlenecks.
Targeted changes such as compilation, caching, fused operations, or memory improvements.
A performance report comparing latency and memory before and after the changes.

best_practices: Provide target framework, parameter limits, memory limits, and expected input shapes.
Review the implementation plan before allowing file edits or command execution.
Ask for tests and performance metrics that match your deployment environment.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for a full research implementation without sharing the paper or architecture details.
Do not skip tests for models that will be trained or deployed later.
Do not run generated training code on sensitive data before reviewing file and command changes.

faq.question: What frameworks does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on PyTorch model implementation, training loops, optimization, and validation workflows.

faq.question: Can it implement research papers?
faq.answer: Yes, when you provide the paper, constraints, target architecture, and expected behavior.

faq.question: Does it write code directly?
faq.answer: The documented workflow uses plan mode and can propose file edits through a specialist agent.

faq.question: Can it run tests?
faq.answer: The skill is designed to use validation and test execution when the user permits command execution.

faq.question: Is this safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution because the specialist agent may read, edit, and test project files during implementation.

faq.question: What should I provide for best results?
faq.answer: Provide requirements, existing code, data shapes, hardware limits, metrics, and any reference material.
